_cite.reason: 4c: relabel unlabeled workspace-analysis (c3 §1); every binding sentence judged WORKSPACE_ANALYSIS

prior.content_md:
**Format:** Regulatory Fact Document
**Item type:** Standard
**Primary source:** GRI Standards English Language, Global Reporting Initiative, https://www.globalreporting.org/how-to-use-the-gri-standards/gri-standards-english-language/
**Date of source content:** Standards library page reflects publications through June 2025 (Full set of GRI Standards updated 26 Jun 2025; GRI 102: Climate Change 2025 and GRI 103: Energy 2025 published 26 Jun 2025).
**Date of this brief:** April 2026

This document covers the GRI Standards as a voluntary sustainability reporting framework, their structural architecture, and their operational relevance to a freight forwarder operating across air (primary), road (secondary), and ocean (tertiary) modes on Americas, Europe, and Asia trade lanes handling live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian cargo verticals.

**Convention notes:**
- The GRI Standards are voluntary in nature; they impose no binding legal obligations. Every reference to "requirements" in this document refers to the internal requirements of the GRI Standards framework for an organisation that elects to report in accordance with them — not to statutory mandates. Items where voluntary standards intersect with binding regulatory frameworks (CSRD/ESRS, CSRD-GRI alignment) are labeled **Legal Confirmation Required** where legal interpretation is needed.
- The source URL retrieved cookie-consent page content rather than structured standards text. Substantive claims about the Standards' architecture below are drawn from the standards listing visible in the source content and from the GRI Standards page structure as published. Where the source content confirms a specific publication date or document name, that is cited. Where claims depend on the full text of individual standards not retrieved, this is noted as a research gap.

---
